text: This video will demonstrate the benefit of robotic ETEP incisional hernia repair in the patient with a petite torso.
Traditionally, minimally invasive surgery has been very challenging in petite torso. This case is interesting in that the defect size was relatively large for the patient's size.
She was four foot eleven and a hundred twenty-five pounds. The defect is nine centimeters wide by fifteen centimeters long.

Traditionally, transabdominal minimally invasive surgery, whether it's laparoscopic or robotic, is very frustrating at best, but usually is not possible due to the inability for proper port spacing and achieving adequate workspace to secure a large piece of mesh.
This case, I believe, was possible due to the benefits of both robotics and the ETIP reef stopper operation.
Next, you'll see the CT scan. The patient's rectus width was only four point four centimeters.
On CT, the measurement of the defect width was eight point five centimeters. Intra-op, it was about nine centimeters.
The sagittal view shows you that this defect was about fourteen to fifteen centimeters long. You can see this takes up about half of her abdominal length.

The benefits of ETEP include the ability to place ports more medial. It's usually medial to the linea similaris, which means that there's more room for proper port spacing, since the distance between the cossum margin and the iliac crest allows for adequate port spacing. The mesh is also sandwiched in the retro-rectus space.
Rather than needing to be tacked with trans-fascials or tackers, the mesh is just sandwiched in a space and left in there without the need for securing with sutures or tacks.

The surgeon places the mesh in the retro-rectus space, ensuring it is positioned underneath the ports to provide wide overlap over the defect. The robotic assistance enhances the precision of the ETEP operation.

The surgeon operates in the retro-rectus space of a petite patient, where the rectus muscle measures 4.4 centimeters in width, demonstrating the suitability of the procedure for small anatomical spaces.
The surgeon performs defect closure in a tight workspace, with ports positioned close together, avoiding instrument and body collisions.

Also, in these situations I find image aversion very helpful because many times the right hand port is not in a good location for suturing, but with image aversion you can turn the left hand instrument into the right hand for adequate suturing.
The surgeon begins the operation with optical entry in the right upper quadrant, placing three ports medial to the right linea semilunaris.
The surgeon inserts a port into the right lower quadrant, navigating the very small space with difficulty as the port enters the surgical field.
The robotic system is docked in the lower abdomen, where the surgeon has limited space to maneuver within the surgical field.
This was definitely very claustrophobic and I had to make micro-movements in order to not accidentally injure structures with the movement of the robotic instrument.
The surgeon begins the procedure in the upper abdomen, where there is more space available, with both robotic instruments visible in the same view.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the retro-rectus space before initiating the crossover procedure, ensuring the CO-II remains contained within the space for easier manipulation.
The patient had a prior emergent right colectomy for a cecal volvulus. And that's how she developed the incisional hernia.
The surgeon enters the abdominal cavity during the crossover, revealing minimal preperitoneal fat around the hernia site.
In the upper abdomen, the surgeon navigates through a plane with increased pre-peritoneal fat, carefully bringing the fat down within the pre-peritoneal space.
The surgeon is fully intra-abdominal, using instruments to take down adhesions and then proceeding to remove the pseudosac, anticipating a large posterior gap in the defect.
And it's nice to have some type of pseudosac to patch the posterior gap.

The surgeon completes the crossover in the lower abdomen by cutting the patient's right posterior rectal sheath to access the preperitoneal space below the umbilicus, revealing a very tight working space.
The surgeon completes the lysis of adhesions within the hernia sac and enters the left retroactive space, marking the beginning of stage three of the procedure.
The surgeon enters the left retroactive space both below and above the hernia defect, preparing to proceed from left to right.
The surgeon uses a grasper to guide the incision in the posterior rectus sheath, completing the retro-rectus dissection.
The surgeon divides the round ligament in the left pelvis, a straightforward step in the procedure.
The surgeon begins a bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) at the arcuate line to achieve anterior release for closing a large defect in the patient's torso.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum from behind the posterior rectus sheath, preparing to divide the sheath while avoiding excessive traction that could inadvertently pull the anterior lamella of the internal oblique into the transection line.

The surgeon initiates the upper transversus abdominis release (TAR) by dissecting under the diaphragm. The posterior rectus sheath is divided, along with the posterior lamella of the internal oblique, transverse abdominis, and the posterior contribution of the diaphragm.
The surgeon completes the transversus abdominis release (TAR) by connecting the bottoms-up dissection to the top-down dissection under the diaphragm. The camera provides a view of the defect area.
The surgeon encounters difficulty visualizing the ipsilateral linea alba while attempting to close the posterior layer of the defect.
The surgeon measures the defect, approximately nine by fifteen centimeters, within a small workspace. The posterior layer is being closed, visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the pseudo-sac, held in the left hand, to patch a large defect. Despite performing a left-sided TAR, the posterior sheath edges remain separated, and the pseudo-sac effectively fills the gap.
If it wasn't for the Pseudosac, I would have needed a different patch, such as biologic mesh or dissolvable mesh, or perform a right-sided tar.
The surgeon uses a three-hole V-lock 180 suture to close the final defect in the posterior gap, completing the closure process.
The surgeon plans to close the anterior defect by addressing the diastasis and repositioning the camera to achieve a top-down view, preparing to place a port in the left upper quadrant.
The surgeon shifts the instruments to the left upper quadrant port, providing a clear view of the edge of the mesh within the surgical field.

The surgeon performs image aversion to switch hand positions, allowing the left hand to function as the right hand for easier suturing. The right hand now moves freely to reach necessary areas, while the left hand is constrained near the ipsilateral edge, with the port positioned less than four centimeters away.
The surgeon demonstrates defect closure using image inversion, highlighting the limited workspace due to the proximity of the roof at the bow, with robotic assistance facilitating the procedure.
The surgeon places a large piece of mesh to fill the entire space, extending beyond the area of the transversus abdominis release (TAR) to cover the left linea semilunaris.
No drains were placed. Thank you for watching.
